Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of just fit studying in whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Being a kinesthetic learner, I find sitting and reading really boring, so I'd often put things off until I absolutely had to do them. Sometimes I'd study in bursts, like doing a lot in one day and then nothing for a week.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd cram before exams because that's when the pressure actually kicked in, but during regular weeks I barely touched anything. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving. If nothing was due, I'd just drift and do other things with my extracurriculars instead.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I had to check if something was due or if there was an announcement I'd missed. Sometimes my peer support group would message me saying we needed to discuss something, so I'd go check the discussion boards. It wasn't really habit—more like reacting to external pressure or reminders from others.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never felt like something I naturally wanted to open. Maybe for a week or two when we had group activities going on, I'd check it more regularly because my peers were expecting me to respond. But that faded pretty quickly once the activity ended.
Q5: The videos were actually decent because I could watch them while doing other things or moving around. I also found the peer interactions helpful—talking through problems with classmates made things click better than just reading. Discussion posts were okay when people actually engaged, but that was hit or miss.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials or the optional quizzes. They felt so dry and I just couldn't make myself sit through them. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. If it wasn't going to be graded heavily, I just didn't prioritize it.
Q7: If I could see how it would directly help me pass or do well on an exam, then it felt worth it. Also, anything hands-on or interactive kept my attention better. But if it was just passive reading or something that felt like busywork, I'd zone out completely and wonder why I was even bothering.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Like, if it's optional, that's basically the course telling me I don't have to do it, right? Required activities at least had consequences, so I'd make sure to do those—even if I did them last minute. Optional ones just never made it to my priority list.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs and then study from my phone or print them out. I learn better when I can move around, so I'd take notes while pacing or practice problems at a coffee shop without being online. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: When I was preparing for exams, I'd use my own notes and work through practice problems I'd printed out. None of that showed up online. I'd also study with friends in person—we'd quiz each other and explain concepts, but the LMS wouldn't capture any of that collaboration.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once, maybe download it or take screenshots of key parts, and then review those offline later. Once I had what I needed, I didn't see the point in logging back in just to rewatch the same thing. It was more efficient for me that way.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just felt really bored and disconnected from the material. My anxiety would spike sometimes thinking about all the stuff I hadn't done, but that actually made me avoid the LMS more, not less. When extracurriculars got busy, coursework just fell to the bottom of my list.
Q13: Yeah, for sure. When I was tired or stressed about other stuff, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. If I was confused about something, I'd sometimes just give up rather than dig through resources. Competing responsibilities from my activities definitely pulled my attention away from the course.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while scrolling on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd skim through readings without actually absorbing anything. I was technically 'using' the LMS, but my brain was somewhere else completely.
Q15: I put off assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was boredom—the tasks just didn't excite me. Part of it was anxiety, weirdly enough. Like, the more important something was, the more anxious I felt, so I'd avoid it until the last possible moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only reason I engaged at all. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly be all over the LMS, downloading stuff, watching videos at 2x speed, trying to piece things together. Without deadlines, I'd just float along doing the bare minimum.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it gave me a little boost, but it didn't really change my habits much. Bad feedback sometimes made me feel worse and less motivated to try. The peer support helped more than instructor feedback—hearing from classmates felt less intimidating and more relatable.
Q18: The structure felt pretty loose to me, which was both good and bad. Good because I didn't feel suffocated, but bad because I had no external push to stay on track. If there had been more frequent small deadlines or check-ins, I probably would have engaged more consistently.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once made it hard to keep track of everything. The course itself wasn't badly designed, but when you're juggling fourteen courses, things slip through the cracks. More interactive or hands-on elements would've helped me stay engaged instead of zoning out.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—clicking around, opening things, maybe even watching videos, but not actually completing the assignments. You can be present in the system without really being productive. Sometimes it's anxiety, sometimes it's just not knowing where to start.
Q21: That's literally me. I did well on exams because I crammed effectively and I learn fast when I focus. But my LMS activity was pretty low because I studied offline and only engaged with the platform when absolutely necessary. The grades don't always match the clicks.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers in person, and whether you were genuinely engaged or just going through the motions.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling curious about it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and wanting to understand. I didn't feel that way often this semester, honestly.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from my peer support group in person, or how I studied from printed notes while walking around. It also wouldn't capture my anxiety or boredom—the emotional stuff that really affected how I engaged. Data just shows actions, not the reasons behind them.
